title: Mastocytosis
section: The Immune System in Health and Disease
authors: Matthew P. Giannetti, Joshua A. Boyce

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Genetic Basis of Mastocytosis
items.content: Mastocytosis is characterized by an accumulation of clonally expanded mast cells in various tissues, most commonly diagnosed in infancy and young adulthood. The disease has a prevalence of approximately 1 in 10,000 people, with over 90% of patients carrying the KIT D816V mutation.
keyPoints: Mastocytosis involves clonal expansion of mast cells in skin, bone marrow, liver, spleen, and gut.
>90% of patients carry the KIT D816V mutation.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Classification and Prognostic Implications in Systemic Mastocytosis
items.content: Systemic mastocytosis (SM) is classified into five variants, with Indolent systemic mastocytosis (ISM) being the most common in adults. ISM has a normal life expectancy, while aggressive forms like ASM and MCL have poor prognoses.
keyPoints: Indolent systemic mastocytosis (ISM) accounts for >70% of adult patients.
ISM is associated with a normal life expectancy.
Aggressive systemic mastocytosis (ASM) and mast cell leukemia (MCL) have poor prognoses.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Aggressive Systemic Mastocytosis
items.content: Aggressive systemic mastocytosis (ASM) is diagnosed with high mast cell burden and one or more C findings, including cytopenias, hepatomegaly with impaired liver function, palpable splenomegaly with hypersplenism, malabsorption, and skeletal lesions.
keyPoints: High MC burden: Bone marrow infiltration >30%, serum tryptase >200 ng/mL, KITD816V >10% VAF.

C findings for ASM diagnosis include cytopenias (ANC <1000/μL or Hb <10 g/dL or PLT <100,000/μL), hepatomegaly with ascites and impaired liver function, palpable splenomegaly with hypersplenism, malabsorption with hypoalbuminemia and weight loss, skeletal lesions with osteolysis and pathologic fractures.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Darier's Sign in MPCM
items.content: Darier's sign is a key diagnostic feature for mastocytosis, particularly in monomorphic and polymorphic cutaneous mastocytosis (MPCM). It involves the appearance of urtication and erythema following trauma to reddish-brown macules, papules, or plaques.
keyPoints: Darier's sign is characterized by urtication and erythema upon trauma.
Occurs in MPCM with reddish-brown macules, papules, or plaques.
Helps differentiate between polymorphic and monomorphic forms of MPCM.

items.title: Systemic Symptoms Induced by Mastocytoma Lesions
items.content: Rubbing or irritation of mastocytoma lesions can lead to systemic symptoms such as flushing and urticaria. This is due to the release of bioactive substances from mast cells.
keyPoints: Mastocytomas are solitary elevated lesions that may be yellow, brown, or red.
Size varies from a few millimeters to several centimeters.
Systemic symptoms like flushing and urticaria can occur upon lesion irritation.

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with mastocytosis?
items.explanation: The KIT D816V mutation is the most common gain-of-function mutation in patients with mastocytosis.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of mastocytosis?
items.explanation: The prevalence of mastocytosis is estimated at ~1 in 10,000 people.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which variant of systemic mastocytosis accounts for more than 70% of adult patients?
items.explanation: Indolent systemic mastocytosis (ISM) accounts for more than 70% of adult patients with systemic mastocytosis.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which form of cutaneous mastocytosis is characterized by fixed, maculopapular, polymorphic, and hyperpigmented lesions?
items.explanation: Maculopapular cutaneous mastocytosis (MPCM) is characterized by fixed, maculopapular, polymorphic, and hyperpigmented lesions.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a direct cause of clinical manifestations in SM due to increased local mast cell burden?
items.explanation: Gastric acid hypersecretion is a systemic manifestation, not directly caused by increased local mast cell burden.

items.question: Which clinical feature is associated with Darier's sign in MPCM?
items.explanation: Darier's sign refers to the response of cutaneous lesions with urtication and erythema upon trauma.

items.question: Which form of mastocytosis is characterized by generalized skin thickening and a 'peau d'orange' appearance?
items.explanation: Diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis (DCM) presents with generalized skin thickening and a 'peau d'orange' appearance.

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Familial occurrence of mastocytosis is common.
items.explanation: Familial occurrence of mastocytosis is rare.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Progression from indolent systemic mastocytosis to a more advanced form is common.
items.explanation: Progression from ISM to a more advanced form is uncommon, with a ~5% lifetime risk.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Anaphylaxis in SM is most commonly induced by Hymenoptera stings.
items.explanation: Hymenoptera stings are a common trigger for anaphylaxis in patients with SM.

items.statement: Mast cell-mediated fibrotic changes occur in the gastrointestinal tissue.
items.explanation: Fibrotic changes due to mast cells do not occur in gastrointestinal tissue.

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.assertion: Patients with indolent systemic mastocytosis have a normal life expectancy.
items.reason: Indolent systemic mastocytosis is diagnosed when there is no evidence of organ dysfunction due to mast cell infiltration, an associated hematologic disorder, or mast cell leukemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with ISM have a normal life expectancy.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.assertion: Progression from cutaneous mastocytosis to indolent systemic mastocytosis is common in children.
items.reason: Children with high mast cell burden or hematologic abnormalities are at risk of progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not make progression from cutaneous mastocytosis to ISM common; it only indicates increased risk in certain cases.
items.reference: Mastocytosis · Mastocytosis | DEFINITION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
